'''Rhyse Martin''' (born 1 March 1993) is a [[Papua New Guinea national rugby league team|Papua New Guinea]] international [[rugby league]] footballer who plays as a {{rlp|sr}} forward for [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] in the [[Betfred Super League]].<ref name="MartinKRSigns">{{cite news|title=Hull KR confirm signing of Rhinos forward Martin|url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/rugby-league/articles/c2dg71pp63do|access-date=28 August 2024|work=BBC Sport |date=28 August 2024 }}</ref> 

He previously played for the [[Leeds Rhinos]] in the [[Super League]] and the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]] in the [[National Rugby League|NRL]], as well as captaining [[Papua New Guinea national rugby league team|Papua New Guinea]] at international level.

==Background==
Martin was born in [[Cairns, Queensland]], [[Australia]], and is of [[Papua New Guinean]] descent through his father, who is from Hula in the [[Central Province (Papua New Guinea)|Central Province]].<ref name="Kumuls">{{cite web|url=http://kumuls.pngfacts.com/news/png-pms-xiii-recruit-rhyse-martin-raring-to-go|title=PNG PM's XIII recruit, Rhyse Martin raring to go|website=Papua New Guinea Kumuls|date=10 June 2014|access-date=9 October 2016}}</ref>

Martin was raised in the [[Whitsundays]]. As a teenager, Martin moved to [[Townsville, Queensland|Townsville]], where he attended [[Kirwan State High School]] and played for Townsville Brothers.{{cn|date=June 2025}}

He played his junior rugby league for the Proserpine Brahmans.<ref name="DTS">{{Cite news|url=https://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/nrl/teams/bulldogs/canterbury-in-danger-of-losing-boom-backrower-rhyse-martin/news-story/4813555a96a5189bb513270dfba37c04 |title=Bulldogs in danger of losing Martin
|work=[[The Daily Telegraph (Sydney)|The Daily Telegraph]] |location=Sydney |date= |access-date=5 May 2019|url-access=subscription}}</ref>{{better source needed|reason=Source is completely inaccessible without subscription.|date=February 2026}} After graduating in 2010, Martin moved to [[Sydney]] to join the [[Sydney Roosters]].<ref name="Townsville2014">{{cite news |last1=Thompson |first1=Michael |title=Former Sydney Roosters player Rhyse Martin sets himself NRL goal at Townsville Blackhawks |url=https://www.townsvillebulletin.com.au/sport/former-sydney-roosters-player-rhyse-martin-sets-himself-nrl-goal-at-townsville-blackhawks/news-story/fa8702e329c98f9f8ea35064d2135197 |access-date=9 October 2016 |work=Townsville Bulletin |date=5 November 2014}}</ref>

==Club career==
===Early career===
In 2011, Martin played for the [[Sydney Roosters]] [[SG Ball Cup]] side.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.ourfootyteam.com/2011_nswrl_junior_rep_teamLists.php|title=2011 NSWRL Junior Rep's team lists|website=www.ourfootyteam.com|access-date=9 October 2016|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160823041254/http://www.ourfootyteam.com/2011_nswrl_junior_rep_teamLists.php|archive-date=23 August 2016}}</ref> Martin played for the Roosters [[National Youth Competition (rugby league)|NYC]] team in 2012 and 2013, captaining the side.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.roosters.com.au/news/2013/02/16/rhyse-martin-called-into-nrl-squad.html|title=Rhyse Martin called into NRL squad|publisher=Sydney Roosters|date=16 February 2013|access-date=9 October 2016}}</ref> On 20 April 2013, Martin was selected to represent the [[Queensland under-20 rugby league team]] against the New South Wales Under-20s rugby league team, playing off the interchange bench in the 36-12 loss at [[Penrith Stadium]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2013/04/15/under-20s-origin-squads-named/|title=Under 20's Origin squads named|date=15 April 2013|work=National Rugby League|access-date=9 October 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=NSW maintain hold on Under 20 shield |url=https://www.qrl.com.au/news/2016/07/13/nsw-maintain-hold-on-under-20-shield/ |publisher=Queensland Rugby League |access-date=7 February 2026 |date=13 July 2016}}</ref> 

[[File:Rhyse Martin.jpg|thumb|right|Martin playing for the Newtown Jets in 2014]]

In 2014, Martin joined the Roosters' [[National Rugby League|NRL]] squad but did not make his first grade debut, spending the season playing for their feeder club, the [[Newtown Jets]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.roosters.com.au/news/2014/03/25/newtown_jets_team_naming.af_video_hard_effort.html|title=Newtown Jets Team Naming|publisher=Sydney Roosters|date=25 March 2014|access-date=9 October 2016|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161010182242/http://www.roosters.com.au/news/2014/03/25/newtown_jets_team_naming.af_video_hard_effort.html|archive-date=10 October 2016}}</ref> The following November, Martin returned to North Queensland, joining the newly formed [[Intrust Super Cup]] team, the [[Townsville Blackhawks]].<ref name="Townsville2014" /> 

===Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs===
On 14 September 2016, Martin signed a two-year deal with the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.qrl.com.au/news/2016/09/14/martin_signs_with_bu.html|title=Martin signs with Bulldogs|publisher=Queensland Rugby League|date=14 September 2016|access-date=9 October 2016}}</ref> Martin spent 2017 playing for the Bulldogs [[New South Wales Cup|NSW Cup]] team.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.bulldogs.com.au/news/2017/09/20/rhyse-martin-2017-highlights/|title=Rhyse Martin 2017 Highlights|access-date=5 May 2019|publisher=Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs |date=20 September 2017 }}</ref> 

After showing good form for the Canterbury NSW Cup team, Martin finally had the chance to make his NRL debut at 25 years old. In round 9 of the [[2018 NRL season]], Martin made his NRL debut for the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]] against the [[Brisbane Broncos]], starting at second-row and scoring a try with his first ever touch in Canterbury's last-second 22-20 loss at [[Suncorp Stadium]].<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Gould|first1=Joel|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2018/05/03/brisbane-broncos-fight-back-from-half-time-deficit-to-beat-canterbury-bulldogs/|title=Broncos beat luckless Bulldogs courtesy of controversial penalty|date=3 May 2018|work=National Rugby League|access-date=5 May 2019}}</ref>

After the mid-season departure of [[Moses Mbye]] to the [[Wests Tigers]] while Martin was cementing a starting spot at second-row, Martin took over the goal kicking duties. In Canterbury's round 17 loss against the [[Canberra Raiders]] on 7 July 2018 at [[Belmore Oval]], Martin became the first NRL player to score 24 points in a losing side, scoring three tries in the second half and kicking six goals in a match that ended 32-28 after Canterbury had initially led 28-14 with six minutes to full-time.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=McDonald|first1=Margie|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2018/07/08/canterbury-bulldogs-rookie-rhyse-martin-a-points-packing-forward/|title=Record points haul bittersweet for Martin|date=8 July 2018|work=National Rugby League|access-date=5 May 2019}}</ref> 

On 23 July 2018, Martin re-signed with Canterbury-Bankstown for a year to the end of the 2018 season.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.bulldogs.com.au/news/2018/07/23/rhyse-martin-re-signs-with-the-bulldogs/|title=Rhyse Martin re-signs with the Bulldogs|date=23 July 2018|website=Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs|access-date=23 July 2018}}</ref> Before the club's round 23 match against the [[New Zealand Warriors]], Martin made a four-second cameo{{clarify|date=February 2026}} for Canterbury's NSW Cup team against the [[North Sydney Bears]], all because Martin played in seven matches for Canterbury's NSW Cup team before being elevated to first-grade but needed eight ISP appearances so he can play in the NSW Cup Finals series.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=St John|first1=Mark|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/rhyse-martin-has-made-a-two-second-cameo-for-the-bulldogs-isp-side/news-story/b98d7d1937aabfa451b18dace9eaa445|title=Rhyse Martin has made a two second cameo for the Bulldogs ISP side|date=18 August 2018|work=Fox Sports|access-date=5 May 2019}}</ref>

Martin finished his debut year in the NRL with him playing in 14 matches, scoring four tries and produced the greatest goalkicking for the season, converting 36 goals from 38 attempts at an 94.7% and being the highest point-scorer with 88 for the club in the [[2018 NRL season]].<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Kennedy|first1=Chris|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2018/08/24/stat-attack-is-rhyse-martin-best-goal-kicker-in-nrl-era/|title=Stat Attack: Is Martin best goal-kicker in NRL era?|date=24 August 2018|work=National Rugby League|access-date=5 May 2019}}</ref>

As the Canterbury first-grade squad didn't make the finals, Martin continued to play for the club's NSW Cup team, being the Captain and helping them make it into the NSW Cup Grand Final. On 23 September 2018, Martin played at second-row in the Canterbury-Bankstown NSW Cup Grand Final against [[Newtown Jets|Newtown]], kicking 3 goals in the 18-12 victory at [[Leichhardt Oval]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/teams/bulldogs/canterbury-bulldogs-beat-newtown-jets-to-win-2018-intrust-super-premiership-grand-final/news-story/5194d899167222fcbc817a6dfe96cf09|title=Canterbury Bulldogs beat Newtown Jets to win 2018 Intrust Super Premiership grand final|date=23 September 2018|work=Fox Sports|access-date=5 May 2019}}</ref> In the following week, on 30 September 2018, Martin played at second-row in the State Championship Final against [[Queensland Cup]] winners the [[Redcliffe Dolphins]], scoring the first try of the match and kicking five goals in the 42-18 win.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Kennedy|first1=Chris|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2018/09/30/bulldogs-defeat-redcliffe-dolphins-to-win-intrust-super-championship/|title=Bulldogs down Dolphins to win Intrust Super Championship|date=30 September 2018|website=National Rugby League|access-date=5 May 2019}}</ref> On 17 December 2018, Martin was named in the emerging [[Queensland rugby league team|Queensland]] squad.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.couriermail.com.au/sport/nrl/state-of-origin/brisbane-duo-david-fifita-and-jaydn-sua-named-in-emerging-maroons-squad/news-story/eb06bc8bfe4dccad9af61f46b8bc6fdd |title=Titans dominate Emerging Maroons |work=Courier Mail|date=17 December 2018|access-date=5 May 2019|url-access=subscription}}</ref>{{better source needed|reason=Source is completely inaccessible without subscription.|date=February 2026}}

After being one of Canterbury's shining lights from last season, Martin was not selected for the squad in the first two matches of the [[2019 NRL season]].{{cn|date=June 2025}} It was understood that Martin has fallen foul of the coaching staff after he returned to pre-season training carrying a little too much condition and speculation of concerns with his defence.<ref name="DTS"/>{{better source needed|reason=Source is completely inaccessible without subscription.|date=February 2026}} Martin would return in Round 3 against the [[Wests Tigers]] for the demoted [[Raymond Faitala-Mariner]], starting at lock and kicking three goals in Canterbury's 22-8 win at [[Campbelltown Stadium]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/pay-celebrates-new-deal-with-upset-win-over-tigers-20190331-p519cd.html|title=Bulldogs mark Pay's new deal with upset win over Tigers|first=Adam|last=Pengilly|date=31 March 2019|newspaper=[[The Sydney Morning Herald]]|access-date=5 May 2019|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

===Leeds Rhinos===
Martin requested a release from his Canterbury contract in June 2019 after falling out of favour with coach [[Dean Pay]],<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Whaley|first1=Pamela|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/teams/bulldogs/bulldogs-forward-rhyse-martin-granted-shock-release-hours-before-deadline/news-story/9d3bea00e3a8832ede3ab58b53b43588|title=Bulldogs forward Rhyse Martin granted shock release hours before deadline|date=30 June 2019|website=Fox Sports|access-date=30 June 2019}}</ref> and on 30 June, signed a two-and-a-half year deal to join English [[Super League]] club [[Leeds Rhinos]] with immediate effect.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Sadler |first1=Martyn |title=Rhinos confirm signing Rhyse Martin |url=https://www.totalrl.com/rhinos-confirm-signing-rhyse-martin/ |access-date=7 February 2026 |work=Total Rugby League |date=30 June 2019|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

On 17 October 2020, Martin played in Leeds' [[2020 Challenge Cup Final]] victory over [[Salford Red Devils]] at [[Wembley Stadium]], scoring two out of three conversions and contributing to Leeds' first Challenge Cup victory since 2015.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Scott|first1=Ged|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/54555957|title=Leeds beat Salford 17-16 after Luke Gale lands late winning drop-goal|work=BBC Sport |date=15 October 2020 |access-date=15 November 2020}}</ref> Martin made 25 appearances for Leeds in the [[2021 Super League season]], including in the semi-final defeat by [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]], as the club made it to within one game of the [[2021 Super League Grand Final]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Smith |first1=Peter |title=St Helens 36 Leeds Rhinos 8: Hosts too good as semi-final loss ends Rhinos season |url=https://www.yorkshireeveningpost.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/season-over-for-rhinos-as-saints-prove-too-good-3404871 |access-date=7 February 2026 |work=Yorkshire Evening Post |date=1 October 2021 |location=Leeds}}</ref>

In round 22 of the [[2022 Super League season]], Martin scored two tries and kicked seven goals in Leeds 34-14 victory over Salford Red Devils.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/62410758|title=Super League: Leeds Rhinos 34-14 Salford Red Devils - Rhyse Martin stars in Rhinos victory |work=BBC Sport |date=7 August 2022 |access-date=8 August 2022}}</ref> On 24 September 2022, Martin played in the Leeds squad which lost 24-12 to St Helens in the [[2022 Super League Grand Final]] at [[Old Trafford]], scoring a second-half try and kicking two goals during the match.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Bower |first1=Aaron |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2022/sep/24/st-helens-leeds-super-league-grand-final-match-report-rugby-league/|title=St Helens sink Leeds to win record fourth consecutive Grand Final |work=The Guardian |location=London |date=24 September 2022 |access-date=29 September 2022}}</ref> 

Martin played a total of 27 games for Leeds in the 2023 Super League season as the club finished 8th on the table and missed the playoffs. Martin also finished with ten tries and as the clubs top point scorer with 200 points;<ref>{{cite news|last1=Smith|first1=Peter|url=https://www.yorkshireeveningpost.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/leeds-rhinos-2023-season-review-picking-out-the-best-and-worst-players-performances-results-4355920/|title=Leeds Rhinos' 2023 season review: picking out the best and worst players, performances, results|work=Yorkshire Evening Post |location=Leeds |date=2 October 2023 |access-date=9 October 2023}}</ref> in round 16 of the [[2023 Super League season]], he scored two tries and kicked nine goals as Leeds defeated the [[Huddersfield Giants]] 54-0.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/65974502|title=Super League: Leeds Rhinos 54-0 Huddersfield Giants - Hosts pile misery on Ian Watson's side|work=BBC Sport |date=23 June 2023 |access-date=27 June 2023}}</ref> In his final season for Leeds in 2024, on 1 September, Martin scored a [[hat-trick]] and kicked four goals in Leeds' 21-20 golden point extra-time victory over the [[London Broncos]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/c4gq44el41zo|title=Leeds snatch golden-point win over London|work=BBC Sport |date=1 September 2024 |access-date=2 September 2024}}</ref>

===Hull Kingston Rovers===
[[File:Hull KR vs Castleford Tigers, 14-2-2025 Conversion Kick 05 (cropped).jpg|thumb|Martin preparing for a conversion kick for [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] in February 2025]]
Martin confirmed his departure from Leeds at the end of the 2024 on 29 July,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Martin set to leave the Rhinos at the end of the season |url=https://www.therhinos.co.uk/article/20707/martin-set-to-leave-the-rhinos-at-the-end-of-the-season |date=29 July 2024 |access-date=30 July 2024 |publisher=Leeds Rhinos}}</ref> and on 28 August, Martin signed a two-year deal to join [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] in the [[2025 Super League season]].<ref name="MartinKRSigns" />

Martin was part of the squad that brought Hull Kingston Rovers its historic first [[treble (rugby league)|treble]] in 2025; despite being unable for selection for the [[2025 Challenge Cup final]] due to a quad injury sustained earlier in March,<ref>{{cite news |last1=Shaw |first1=Matthew |title=Hull KR receive welcome injury news as verdict delivered on Rhyse Martin return |url=https://www.hulldailymail.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/hull-kr-injury-rhyse-martin-10301715 |access-date=7 February 2026 |work=Hull Daily Mail |date=27 June 2025 |quote=Rovers were boosted by the return of fellow back-rower Rhyse Martin, who was back after recovering from a quad injury that came with a four-month lay-off.}}</ref> Martin played in Hull Kingston Rovers' final round victory on 18 September over [[Warrington Wolves]], kicking four conversions in the fixture that ensured they lifted the [[League Leaders Shield]],<ref>{{cite news |last1=Brennan |first1=Stuart |title=Hull KR see off Wire to win League Leaders' Shield |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/c39rzxxpyjeo |access-date=6 November 2025 |work=BBC Sport |date=18 September 2025}}</ref> and on 9 October, Martin was part of the Hull Kingston Rovers squad that won the [[2025 Super League Grand Final]] and secured their treble at Old Trafford over [[Wigan Warriors]], scoring three conversions during the fixture.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Freeman|first1=Jay|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/cx2jw497jpzo/|title=Hull KR beat Wigan in Grand Final to complete treble|work=BBC Sport|date=11 October 2025|access-date=13 October 2025}}</ref> Also that season, on 31 July, Martin scored a total 22 points by scoring seven conversions and scoring two tries in a 74-12 win over Salford Red Devils, which saw him pass the 1,000 point mark in the Super League, becoming the first Papua New Guinean player to do so.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/ce87xgjlxjdo|title=Four tries for Burgess as Hull KR thrash Salford |work=BBC Sport |date=31 July 2025 |access-date=13 October 2025}}</ref>

On 6 February, Martin broke Hull Kingston Rovers' record for the highest goal scorer in a single fixture, scoring 16 conversions in a [[2026 Challenge Cup]] Round 3 fixture against [[Castleford Lock Lane|Lock Lane ARLFC]], beating a record of 14 goals jointly held by [[Colin Armstrong]], [[Alf Carmichael]], [[Damien Couturier]] and [[Mike Fletcher (rugby league)|Mike Fletcher]]. The fixture, which ended 104-0, also broke Hull Kingston Rovers' record for a highest score in a single match.<ref>{{cite news |last1=McAllister |first1=Josh |title=Hull KR set new club record as Challenge Cup title defence starts in emphatic fashion |url=https://www.hulldailymail.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/hull-kr-set-new-club-10801068 |access-date=6 February 2026 |work=Hull Daily Mail |date=6 February 2026}}</ref>
On 19 February 2026, Martin played in Hull Kingston Rovers [[World Club Challenge]] victory against [[Brisbane Broncos|Brisbane]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/live/cwy6dlgx3kyt/|title=Relive Hull KR's World Club Challenge win over Brisbane|website=www.bbc.co.uk}}</ref>

On 28 May 2026, Martin announced he'll be retiring following the end if the season.<ref>https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/cd0pmxgzppko</ref> Hours later the PNG Chiefs announced that Martin would join the team in 2027 as Football Operations Manager.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2026-05-28 |title=Kumuls star Rhyse Martin joins PNG Chiefs football department |url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2026/05/28/kumuls-star-rhyse-martin-joins-png-chiefs-football-department/ |access-date=2026-05-28 |website=National Rugby League |language=en}}</ref>

==International career==
On 19 October 2014, Martin made his international debut for [[Papua New Guinea national rugby league team|Papua New Guinea]] against [[Tonga national rugby league team|Tonga]], playing at second-row and kicking two goals in the 32-18 win at [[Lae]].

On 2 May 2015, Martin played for Papua New Guinea against [[Fiji national rugby league team|Fiji]] in the [[Melanesian Cup]], playing at second-row in the 22-10 loss at [[Robina Stadium]].<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Webeck|first1=Tony|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2015/05/02/koroibete-delights-in-fiji-win-over-png/|title=Koroibete delights in Fiji win over PNG|date=2 May 2015|work=National Rugby League|access-date=9 October 2016}}</ref> In May 2016, Martin was selected again for Papua New Guinea for the Melanesian Cup but withdrew for personal reasons.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://news.pngfacts.com/2016/05/kumuls-martin-withdraws-goma-in.html|title=Kumuls Martin withdraws, Goma in|work=Papua New Guinea Today|date=2 May 2016|access-date=9 October 2016}}</ref> Martin again played for the Townsville Blackhawks in 2016 and was named at second-row in the 2016 Intrust Super Cup team of the year.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.qrl.com.au/news/2016/08/31/chudleigh_wins_couri.html|title=Chudleigh wins Courier-Mail Medal|publisher=Queensland Rugby League|date=31 August 2016|access-date=9 October 2016}}</ref>

Martin played in all four matches for Papua New Guinea, scoring two tries and kicking 16 goals in their [[2017 Rugby League World Cup]] campaign.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Blyth|first1=Jack|url=https://www.zerotackle.com/rhyse-martin-2017-world-cups-first-breakout-player-30080/|title=Rhyse Martin, the 2017 World Cup's first breakout player|date=28 October 2017|work=Zero Tackle|access-date=5 May 2019}}</ref>

On 23 June 2018, Martin played for Papua New Guinea against Fiji in the Melanesian Cup, playing at second-row and kicking five goals in the 26-14 win at [[Campbelltown Stadium]].<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Stanton|first1=Tanisha|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2018/06/23/papua-new-guinea-start-fast-to-stun-fiji-in-pacific-test/|title=Papua New Guinea starts fast to stun Fiji in Pacific Test|date=23 June 2018|work=National Rugby League|access-date=5 May 2019}}</ref>

On 6 October 2018, Martin played for Papua New Guinea against [[Prime Minister's XIII]], playing at second-row, scoring a try and kicking three goals in the 34-18 loss at [[Port Moresby]].<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Chammas|first1=Michael|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2018/10/06/prime-ministers-xiii-score-hard-earned-win-over-papua-new-guinea-pms-xiii/|title=After pitch invasion, PM's XIII prevail|date=6 October 2018|work=National Rugby League|access-date=5 May 2019}}</ref>

Playing for Papua New Guinea in the second round of the group stage in the delayed [[2021 Rugby League World Cup]], Martin had the chance to set a new world record for most consecutive conversions in a row; Martin had previously kicked 41 successful goals since July 2022. However, Martin missed a conversion attempt to set the new world record during Papua New Guinea's 32-16 victory over the [[Cook Islands national rugby league team|Cook Islands]].<ref>{{cite news|last1=Walker|first1=Callum|url=https://www.totalrl.com/leeds-rhinos-star-rhyse-martin-falls-agonisingly-short-in-world-record-attempt-in-world-cup-clash/|title=Leeds Rhinos star Rhyse Martin falls agonisingly short in world-record attempt in World Cup clash|work=Total Rugby League|date=25 October 2022|access-date=26 October 2022|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

==Personal life==
Martin has four older brothers and one younger brother. One of his older brothers, [[Tyson Martin|Tyson]], played for the [[Mackay Cutters]] and was also a [[Papua New Guinea national rugby league team|Papua New Guinean]] international.<ref name="Kumuls" />
